data.title: Even Trump can't escape the presidential foreign-policy trap
data.description:
More often than not, Americans elect presidents based on domestic issues. But presidents are inevitably dragged into foreign policy issues and problems. President Trump is no exception.

Fittingly for Trump, his situation is more extreme than most. Elected under the banner of “America First,” Trump has not so much been dragged into foreign affairs as he has enthusiastically embraced it. And that could prove dangerous. 

The American public does tend to support the president in national security crises. But the reality is that, absent the perception of a real national security threat, Americans tune out international affairs. Americans are insular. When asked to rank their top issue, foreign policy consistently lags issues like inflation, the economy  and health care — usually barely topping 1 percent.

And this makes sense. The U.S. is less connected to outside countries than any other developed economy. As a percentage of GDP, its trade (imports and exports) is at a lower percentage than for any other developed economy, and most undeveloped ones. Sourced from World Bank data, imports were just 15 percent of GDP, nearly 10 points less than Japan. And exports were under 12 percent — 21 points below France, which is lowest in the European Union. 

This disconnect gives presidents a lot of leeway in foreign policy. As long as the economy is doing well, international affairs are not a problem. And when the economy goes bad — see Jimmy Carter and George H.W. Bush — no amount of international success helps.

Alternatively, presidents whose foreign adventures turn into a quagmire can see their presidencies crippled (see Lyndon Johnson and George W. Bush). 

Thus, the challenge for Trump is not just to make his foreign policy work, but — much more importantly — to make hiw domestic policy succeed. 

RealClearPolitics averages overall approval and by issue. At the time of writing this piece, Trump was net negative 15 points in overall approval, and down 7 points in the Trump-favorable Rasmussen poll. His foreign policy approval was down to a similar level. But polling on inflation is especially bad — down 23 points. And he is down 15 points on the economy in general. 

On the two domestic issues where Trump has been most focused, his numbers are much better. Despite the controversy in Minnesota, Trump is only down 4 percent on immigration on average, and just 1.3 percent on crime. Both Morning Consult and RMG Research have Trump net positive in their most recent polls on immigration. 

Looking at the most recent YouGov benchmark (a somewhat Trump-unfavorable survey), the poll tells the same story. Trump’s personal approval is negative 15 points, but he performs 8 points better on crime and 5 points better on immigration. On inflation, he’s12 points worse.  

The problem Trump has is that his best issues are not the top concern of the overall public and independents (i.e., swing voters). Immigration was the top issue of 12 percent of the public and just 6 percent of independents. Crime is a lowly 2 percent (4 percent for independents). Meanwhile, inflation was at 22 percent and 25 percent, respectively. 

The more Trump-favorable Wall Street Journal poll has the president’s favorability at negative 7 points. But again, his domestic policy numbers are a problem. He performs 3 points worse on the economy, 5 points worse on health care and 10 points worse on inflation. Confirming other polls, Trump is 3 points better on immigration and 6 points net positive on “border security.” 

The curious thing is that Trump is getting a bad rap on inflation. The Economist, a strongly anti-Trump publication, did a deep dive into the numbers and found that inflation has cooled significantly under Trump. Even more importantly, real incomes (income adjusted for inflation) are rising under Trump — and rising more for lower-income Americans. 

The problem for Trump is not entirely of his own making. After years of tame inflation, the shock under former President Joe Biden was severe. And the bigger the shock to the public, the longer it takes to get used to the new normal.

Trump has not ignored the issue; he has proposed banning private equity from owning homes, presumably to force more supply into the market. He has worked to get deals for cheaper drugs and put caps on credit card interest rates. 

But the problem for Trump is twofold.  

First, there is not a coherent policy he is promoting. It’s a lot of opportunism. Cutting housing costs requires a broad set of policy moves, as does reining in health care costs. Trump needs to announce a big set of initiatives in each area, not drip out one-off deals. 

The second problem is one of emphasis. Each of Trump’s recent shots at inflation have been swamped by his foreign policy moves. His proposal to cap credit card interest rates has been drowned by talk of acquiring Greenland, something never even broached in his 2024 campaign. And support is weak, with just 29 percent in favor (YouGov poll), including just 58 percent of Republicans — a very low number for Trump. 

For nearly four years, the American public has identified inflation as the No.1 issue, and by overwhelming numbers. Joe Biden and Kamala Harris ignored it. If Trump continues to allow himself to be distracted away from tackling inflation, he is likely to pay a big price. 

Keith Naughton, a longtime Republican political consultant, is co-founder of Silent Majority Strategies, a public and regulatory affairs consulting firm, and a former Republican political campaign consultant in Pennsylvania.

data.title: Live updates: Jack Smith testifies in House; major allies absent at Trump's Board of Peace ceremony
data.description:
Former special counsel Jack Smith is testifying on Capitol Hill Thursday morning, in his first public testimony about his investigations into President Trump. 

Smith, per his opening remarks, will stand by his decision to probe Trump over efforts to overturn the 2020 election and his mishandling of classified documents

“No one should be above the law in our country, and the law required that [Trump] be held to account. So that is what I did,” Smith will say in his remarks. 

Trump is on his way back to the U.S. from Davos, Switzerland, where he met with world leaders and unveiled his new Board of Peace in Gaza at the annual World Economic Forum. 

Earlier Thursday, the president participated in a signing ceremony for his peace board initiative alongside a handful of world leaders. No major European allies attended the Thursday signing ceremony, however, as tensions flare with NATO amid Trump’s bid to acquire Greenland

After he delivered remarks Wednesday, Trump announced that allies reached a “framework” of a deal on his quest to acquire Greenland. The president also said he would no longer impose tariffs on Denmark and other NATO countries, walking back threats to forcefully take over the island nation.

Trump also huddled with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky Thursday morning in closed door meeting, amid his efforts to bring about an end to Russia’s nearly four-year-war in Ukraine.

Vice President Vance, meanwhile, will travel to Toledo, Ohio, and then Minneapolis, where protests have flared over federal immigration enforcement efforts across the state.

Follow live below.

data.title: Trump predicts 'Democrat shutdown' as funding deadline looms
data.description:
President Trump predicted in an interview on Thursday that the U.S. was likely headed toward another shutdown before the funding deadline at the end of January.

“I think we have a problem, because I think we’re going to probably end up in another Democrat shutdown,” Trump told host Maria Bartiromo on Fox Business’s “Mornings with Maria.”

“The shutdown cost us a lot, and I think they’ll probably do it again, that’s my feeling. We’ll see what happens,” he continued. 

Lawmakers in the House and the Senate have until Jan. 30 to avoid a government shutdown. So far, they have passed six out of the 12 appropriations bills needed to fund the government. Last week the House passed an additional two-bill package known as a minibus that would fund the State Department and the Treasury Department.  Senators will take that minibus up after they come back from their recess next week.

So far, there appears to be little appetite among Democrats for another shutdown.

However, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) appropriations bill has become a significant point of contention between both parties.

Democrats in the House and the Senate have called for an overhaul of Immigration Customs Enforcement after an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer fatally shot an unarmed woman in Minneapolis. Democrats say they will oppose the DHS bill unless it includes tougher oversight and conduct guidelines for ICE officers. 

The last government shutdown began in the beginning of October and went on for 43 days. That shutdown was over extending Affordable Care Act, or Obamacare subsidies.

data.title: 'Sinners' sets Oscars record with 16 nominations
data.description:
Ryan Coogler’s blues-steeped vampire epic “Sinners” led all films with 16 nominations to the 98th Academy Awards on Thursday, setting a record for the most in Oscar history.

Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences voters showered “Sinners” with more nominations than they had ever bestowed before, breaking the 14-nomination mark set by “All About Eve,” “Titanic” and “La La Land.” Along with best picture, Coogler was nominated for best director and best screenplay, and double-duty star Michael B. Jordan was rewarded with his first Oscar nomination, for best actor.

Paul Thomas Anderson’s father-daughter revolutionary saga “One Battle After Another,” the favorite coming into nominations, trailed in second with 13 nominations of its own. Four of its actors — Leonardo DiCaprio, Teyana Taylor, Benicio del Toro and Sean Penn — were nominated, though newcomer Chase Infiniti was left out in best actress.

In those two top nominees, the film academy put its full force behind a pair of visceral and bracingly original American epics that each connected with a fraught national moment. Coogler’s Jim Crow-era film — the rare horror movie to win the academy’s favor — conjures a mythical allegory of Black life. In “One Battle After Another,” a dormant spirit of rebellion is revived in an out-of-control police state.

Both are also Warner Bros. titles. In the midst of a contentious sale to Netflix, the 102-year-old studio had its best Oscar nominations morning ever. As the fate of Warner Bros., which Netflix is buying for $72 billion, hangs in the balance amid a challenge from Paramount Skydance, Hollywood is bracing for potentially the largest realignment in the film industry’s history.

The 10 films nominated for best picture are “Bugonia,” “F1,” “Frankenstein,” “Hamnet,” “Marty Supreme,” “One Battle After Another,” “The Secret Agent,” “Sentimental Value,” “Sinners” and “Train Dreams.”

Guillermo del Toro’s “Frankenstein,” Josh Safdie’s “Marty Supreme” and Joachim Trier’s “Sentimental Value” all scored nine nominations.

The nine for “Marty Supreme” included a third best actor nod for 30-year-old Timothée Chalamet, the favorite in the category he narrowly missed winning last year for “A Complete Unknown.” With Jordan and Chalamet, the nominees are Leonardo DiCaprio for “One Battle After Another,” Ethan Hawke for “Blue Moon” and Wagner Moura for “The Secret Agent.”

Nominated for best actress was the category favorite, Jessie Buckley (“Hamnet”), along with Rose Byrne (“If I Had Legs I’d Kick You”), Kate Hudson (“Song Sung Blue”), Renate Reinsve (“Sentimental Value”) and two-time winner Emma Stone, who landed her sixth nomination, for “Bugonia.”

The year’s most-watched movie, with more than half a billion views on Netflix, “KPop Demon Hunters,” scored nominations for both best song (“Golden”) and best animated feature. Sony Pictures developed and produced the film, but, after selling it to Netflix, watched it become a worldwide sensation.

Blockbusters otherwise had a difficult morning. Universal Pictures’ “Wicked: For Good” was shut out entirely. While “Avatar: Fire and Ash” notched nominations for costume design and visual effects, it became the first “Avatar” film not nominated for best picture. The biggest box-office hit nominated for Hollywood’s top award instead was “F1,” an Apple production. The streamer partnered with Warner Bros. to distribute the racing drama.

The first category read by presenters Danielle Brooks and Lewis Pullman was supporting actress. The nominees are Elle Fanning and Inga Ibsdotter LilIeaas for “Sentimental Value,” Amy Madigan for “Weapons,” Wunmi Mosaku for “Sinners” and Teyana Taylor for “One Battle After Another.”

For supporting actor, the nominees are Jacob Elordi for “Frankenstein,” Sean Penn for “One Battle After Another,” Stellan Skarsgård for “Sentimental Value,” Benicio del Toro for “One Battle After Another” and Delroy Lindo for “Sinners.”

This year, the Oscars are introducing a new category for casting. That new honor helped “Sinners” and “One Battle After Another” pad their already impressive stats. Along with those two films, the nominees are “Hamnet,” “Marty Supreme” and “The Secret Agent.”

The 98th Academy Awards will take place on March 15 at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles and will be televised live on ABC and Hulu. YouTube’s new deal to exclusively air won’t take effect until 2029. This year, Conan O’Brien will return as host.

data.title: Paris Hilton and Angie K of 'Real Housewives' to make separate advocacy pushes at Capitol on Thursday
data.description:
A former special counsel, a hotel heiress-turned-entrepreneur and a Bravo reality TV star are all poised to be at the Capitol on Thursday for what’s shaping up to be a colorful day.

Both Paris Hilton and “The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City’s” Angie Katsanevas are set to touch down on Capitol Hill for separate events on Thursday. Their visits to the halls of Congress come on the same day that former special counsel Jack Smith is scheduled to publicly testify about his investigations of President Trump. 

Hilton, who became a frequent presence in Washington as she successfully advocated for youth welfare legislation that was signed into law by then-President Biden in 2024, is making a return appearance. 

This time, the singer and DJ will be speaking out at a news conference alongside Reps. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) and Laurel Lee (R-Fla.) to urge lawmakers to pass the Disrupt Explicit Forged Images and Non-Consensual Edits (DEFIANCE) Act. The bill, which unanimously passed the Senate last week, would allow victims of nonconsensual deepfake pornography to sue individuals who produce and distribute such content.

Katsanevas, a popular “Real Housewives” cast member known as “Angie K” on the series, will be trading the Beehive State for the nation’s capital — she’s poised to testify Thursday afternoon at a House Small Business Committee hearing called “Local Ownership, National Brands: How Franchising is a Pathway to Entrepreneurship.”

Katsanevas is the owner and founder of the franchised salon brand, Lunatic Fringe, and according to the International Franchise Association, will advocate for “policies to support the franchise business model.”

data.title: FCC: Late-night show may not be exempt from equal time for opposing candidates
data.description:
The FCC is changing its rules exempting certain late night and daytime talk shows from being required to provide equal airtime to opposing political candidates.

“Importantly, the FCC has not been presented with any evidence that the interview portion of any late night or daytime television talk show program on air presently would qualify for the bona fide news exemption,” the FCC said in a public notice made on Wednesday. “Moreover, a program that is motivated by partisan purposes, for example, would not be entitled to an exemption under longstanding FCC precedent.”

The move comes as Brendan Carr, who was appointed by President Trump, has criticized a number of leading late night and daytime talk shows on major networks like ABC, NBC and CBS.

Carr has said those programs, because they air over a broadcast network rather than a cable channel or streamer, have an obligation to serve the “public interest,” and the FCC intends to hold their networks to that standard.

But critics of the administration argue Trump is using Carr’s FCC to crack down on news and commentary that is critical of his policies and stifling free speech.

During a pair of Congressional hearings in recent weeks, Carr has defended the Trump administration’s FCC policies and argued before lawmakers that his department is not technically an independent government agency.

“It is important that both broadcasters and legally qualified candidates understand the FCC’s equal opportunities regulations and how they can result in broadcasters offering opposing legal qualified candidates comparable time and placement,” the department said in its public notice.

data.title: Trump acts like an imperial bully-in-chief
data.description:
President Trump’s view of international relations can be summarized in three words: “Might makes right.”

Trump clearly agrees with his deputy chief of staff, Stephen Miller, who recently told CNN: “We live in a world … that is governed by strength, that is governed by force, that is governed by power.”

Trump is turning back the clock to the days of imperialism, when the Roman Empire and later England, France, Spain and other great powers — followed by Nazi Germany, the Japanese Empire, fascist Italy and the Soviet Union — imposed their will on weaker adversaries by force.

Americans used force to enslave millions of Africans. America seized land from Native Americans, take what is now the Western U.S. from Mexico, and conquer other territories. And Trump wants to bring back those days of unrestrained American power and expansionism.

He is using the U.S. military to demand other nations do whatever he commands, as if he is emperor of the world. He arrogantly ignores limits that U.S. law, the Constitution and international law place on what he can do.

Asked in a recent New York Times interview if there are any limits on actions he can take to impose his will around the world, Trump responded: “Yeah, there is one thing. My own morality. My own mind. It’s the only thing that can stop me.”

“I don’t need international law,” Trump added. “I’m not looking to hurt people.”

Trump recently ordered the abduction and imprisonment of Nicolas Maduro and his wife in a raid that Venezuela says killed more than 100 people. He claims the right to run that country and control its oil for years, even outrageously calling himself Venezuela’s “acting president” on social media. He had earlier ordered attacks on boats near Venezuela that were allegedly carrying illegal drugs, killing more than 100 people on board without any attempt to arrest them.

Trump’s obsession with grabbing Venezuela’s oil and encouraging more domestic fossil fuel production — while doing all he can to limit the development of clean energy in the U.S. — makes no sense. But he refuses to believe that harmful climate change is caused by oil, natural gas and coal emissions, calling the scientific consensus a “con job,” a “hoax” and a “scam.”

Trump has demanded that Canada become the 51st U.S. state. He says America will retake control of the Panama Canal. He says the U.S. must control Greenland, a semiautonomous territory of NATO member Denmark. Until this week, he had been refusing to take military force off the table to achieve his goal.

Trump has attacked Iran and threatens to do so again. He has ordered attacks on Islamist terrorists in Syria and Nigeria. He has made threats against Cuba, Colombia and Mexico, asserting that the U.S. has the right to dominate the Western Hemisphere under an updated Monroe Doctrine that he calls the Donroe Doctrine.

He has also said America should take ownership of the Gaza Strip and turn it into a tourist resort.

Whatever happened to Trump’s campaign promise to get out of foreign wars and not start new ones? Is he not concerned that his military adventurism could trigger retaliatory strikes and terrorist attacks by his targets that would kill U.S. military members and civilians at home and abroad?

Trump’s aggressive actions could encourage Russian President Vladimir Putin to use military force to conquer not just Ukraine, but other nations once part of the Soviet Union. Chinese President Xi Jinping could be encouraged to invade Taiwan and intimidate other Asian nations.

We don’t know how Trump would respond to such attacks by strongmen he has openly admired and tried to emulate with his own ruthless exercise of power.

Following the deaths of tens of millions of people in World War II and the development of nuclear weapons, nations around the world established the United Nations and strengthened efforts to use international law to limit future conflicts and promote global cooperation. Although there have been many new wars, we have thankfully been spared from World War III.

But Trump has frequently denounced the U.N. and recently withdrawn the U.S. from 31 U.N. agencies and dozens of other global organizations. He has eroded our moral standing in the world by eliminating almost all funding for the U.S. Agency for International Development, which had provided lifesaving medical and food aid and saved millions of lives in poor nations.

Trump has made the world a more dangerous place with incompetent, erratic and dangerous policies based more on his emotions, ego, personal business interests and ignorance than on America’s strategic interests. He has alienated our allies by acting as the global bully in chief and embraced our enemies, most notably Putin.

“Might makes right,” though immoral, anti-democratic and destructive, was the dominant form of international relations for thousands of years. It is frightening that the president of the most powerful nation on Earth seeks to abandon the progress of the post-World War II era, return the world to the deadly and barbaric law of the jungle, and exercise tyrannical control over weaker nations. We deserve better from our president.

A. Scott Bolden is an attorney, NewsNation contributor, former chair of the Washington, D.C. Democratic Party and a former New York state prosecutor.

data.title: Key panel advances three-bill funding package
data.description:
The House Rules Committee on Thursday advanced a three-bill minibus appropriations package, moving the lower chamber one step closer to meeting the Jan. 30 funding deadline.

The panel advanced the package by a recorded vote of 9-4. It would fund the departments of Defense, Housing and Urban Development, Health and Human Services, Labor, Education, Transportation and other related agencies. 

It will now head to the floor for consideration.

During the House Rules Committee hearing on Wednesday, Democrats had raised concerns that the Defense bill in the minibus package didn’t address President Trump’s ongoing threats against Greenland.

Trump has long sought to take over Greenland, a semiautonomous territory of Denmark, and in recent weeks has floated the use of military force to do so. But he reversed course on Wednesday and suggested that the U.S. would not seize the territory by force. He instead called for immediate negotiations for the U.S. to acquire Greenland.

Rep. Jim McGovern (D-Mass.), the ranking member of the Rules Committee, said that the Defense bill was “maybe the last opportunity to prevent this administration from, like, doing something crazy in Greenland or, you know, attacking NATO.”

“I really think that we’re at a moment where we all have to recognize that some of the stuff he’s saying is not only dangerous, it’s just way out there. It’s not normal. And so, yeah, a lot of us believe that just to be safe, there ought to be constraints” in the bill, McGovern said.

Amendments offered by Democrats that sought to address the issue ultimately did not advance for floor consideration.

Internal Republican disputes had also stalled the bills’ advancement through the House Rules Committee on Wednesday.

One major issue GOP leaders faced was requests from midwestern Republicans to authorize year-round sales of E15 ethanol, a fuel blend with 10.5 percent to 15 percent ethanol blended with gasoline that has historically been restricted in the summer due to concerns about smog. 

But GOP leadership reached a deal with the holdouts to create a rural domestic energy council tasking with addressing the authorization of E15 ethanol. The council will be established if the procedural rule on the minibus is adopted. 

Some hard-line conservatives also voiced objections to the package, particularly the earmarks included for various community projects and organizations in local districts. 

“The U.S. is NEARLY $38.5 TRILLION in debt. Families have been cutting back while Washington isn’t,” Rep. Ralph Norman (R-S.C.), who sits on the committee, wrote on X. 

Norman eventually had one of his amendments made in order that would prohibit “funds for certain community funding projects.”

But the package is expected to pass the House with broad bipartisan support. It had been strongly endorsed by House Appropriations Committee Chair Tom Cole (R-Okla.) and ranking member Rosa DeLauro (D-Conn.).

“This latest funding package continues Congress’s forceful rejection of extreme cuts to federal programs proposed by the Trump Administration,” DeLauro said in a statement. “Where the White House attempted to eliminate entire programs, we chose to increase their funding. Where the Administration proposed slashing resources, we chose to sustain funding at current levels.”

Cole said during the House Rules hearing that, “Across these measures, we have real deliverables and real wins.”

data.title: Former Iowa superintendent expected to plead guilty to false citizenship claim
data.description:
Former Des Moines, Iowa, schools Superintendent Ian Roberts is expected to plead guilty Thursday in federal court to charges he made false citizenship claims in employment and illegally possessed firearms.  

Roberts was arrested in September by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), with agents saying he had a removal order since May 2024 and was found in possession of a loaded handgun, which is a crime for those without legal status.

Court documents show Roberts is prepared to plead guilty to lying about his citizenship on employment papers and possession of multiple loaded guns, one in his car and three others at his residence.  

The Hill has reached out to the Department of Homeland Security for comment. 

Roberts, a former Olympic athlete who competed for Guyana, began his two-year tenure as superintendent of Des Moines Public Schools in 2023, where school officials said they had no idea he lied about his citizenship status.  

Before this role, he worked in the education sector in multiple states for two decades. 

Alfredo Parrish, one of Roberts’s attorneys, told the Associated Press that Roberts thought his case was resolved by a previous attorney. 

If convicted, Roberts could face up to 20 years of prison time and be deported after his sentence.

data.title: Smith to condemn 'false' narratives' about Trump investigations
data.description:
Former special counsel Jack Smith will stand by his decision to bring charges against President Trump and “false” narratives around that decision during his public testimony before the House Judiciary Committee Thursday, according to multiple reports. 

“Our investigation developed proof beyond a reasonable doubt that President Trump engaged in criminal activity,” Smith is expected to say Thursday in his opening statement, according to The Associated Press. “If asked whether to prosecute a former president based on the same facts today, I would do so regardless of whether that president was a Republican or a Democrat.”

Smith, an appointee of former President Biden, is testifying at the request of Judiciary Committee Chair Jim Jordan (R-Ohio) on his probes into Trump’s alleged efforts to overturn the 2020 election and his mishandling of classified documents. The hearing is set to begin at 10 a.m. EST.

“No one should be above the law in our country, and the law required that [Trump] be held to account. So that is what I did,” Smith will say, per the AP.

The former prosecutor previously testified for over seven hours in front of the committee on Dec. 17. Jordan’s team released a 255-page transcript of Smith’s deposition on New Year’s Eve.

Throughout that testimony, Smith said he had a strong case against the president. Both investigations, which resulted in the president being indicted in the District of Columbia and Southern District of Florida, were dropped after Trump was elected in 2024. 

At the time, Smith cited the precedent of not bringing charges against a sitting president.

“Our investigation developed proof beyond a reasonable doubt that President Trump engaged in a criminal scheme to overturn the results of the 2020 election and to prevent the lawful transfer of power,” Smith testified in December. “Our investigation also developed powerful evidence that showed that President Trump willfully retained highly classified documents after he left office in January of 2021, storing them at his social club, including in a ballroom and a bathroom. 

“He then repeatedly tried to obstruct justice to conceal his continued retention of those documents,” the former special counsel added.

The public has only seen Smith’s report about his investigation into Trump’s attempts to overturn the 2020 election and block the transfer of power on Jan. 6, 2021. The final report into the 300 classified documents recovered from the president’s Mar-a-Lago estate in Florida remains private.
